findings_summary: The warning frame is strong enough for narration if split into visible industrial placement, water/drainage conditions, prior instability, raised concerns, weak tip policy, and National Coal Board accountability.
The official inquiry digest supports the tribunal frame of clear warnings, lack of direction from above, and Coal Board fault.

The Institution of Civil Engineers source supports the physical mechanism around springs, streams, water buildup, Tip 7, prior instability, absence of governing law, and absence of an NCB tipping policy.
The National Library tribunal PDF URL in the existing audit presented a verification page during this pass, so direct tribunal-page claims should be verified later before final fact-check.
narration_safe_claims: Aberfan was a preventable industrial disaster, not a natural hillside accident.
The visible hazard was coal spoil placed above a village and Pantglas Junior School.
Water, springs, streams, drainage, and saturated spoil are safe to use as the physical failure frame.
Prior instability and raised concerns are safe as warning categories, but detailed chronology should wait for direct tribunal verification.
The National Coal Board responsibility frame is safe, including the tribunal's preventability/accountability posture.
Post-disaster tips law can close the episode as evidence that the category of risk was formalized after consequence.
cautions: Do not invent scenes, dialogue, named local complaints, or exact internal NCB knowledge dates.
Do not make maps or documents the emotional center of the episode.
Do not over-center royal response, media memory, charity-fund disputes, or broad Welsh coal history.
Do not narrate the blocked National Library PDF as directly read in this pass.
next_action: Proceed to a review-ready longform script draft, with later frontier critique and human approval still required before audio.
